metadata.title: The Evolution of Cooperation (Axelrod) — Book Review
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Nik Bear Brown

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Cooperation through reciprocity appears everywhere, from senators trading favors to businesses maintaining quality with suppliers. Across the sequence of chapters, a deeper insight emerges, operating on several levels at once. Acknowledging the strategy's limitations proves as important as celebrating its successes. Simple reciprocity struggles with misunderstanding: one misperceived choice can reverberate into cycles of retaliation.

artifactLines: Cooperation through reciprocity appears everywhere, from senators trading favors to businesses maintaining quality with suppliers.
Across the sequence of chapters, a deeper insight emerges, operating on several levels at once.
Acknowledging the strategy's limitations proves as important as celebrating its successes.
Simple reciprocity struggles with misunderstanding: one misperceived choice can reverberate into cycles of retaliation.

beats.narration_text: Axelrod's point that simple reciprocity can unravel from a single misperceived choice, spiraling into retaliation, is the part I want to explore. Help me: (1) walk through a tit-for-tat exchange where one misread move triggers the spiral, (2) explain what modification, like forgiveness, dampens the cycle, and (3) connect it to a real case such as suppliers maintaining quality or senators trading favors.
